title: Trials, Temptation, and True Religion
subtitle: Count trials as joy, ask God for wisdom, and be doers of the word

sections.header: Verses 1-8 - "Joy in Trials, Wisdom from God"

heb.paragraph: Count it all joy when you meet trials. The command is counterintuitive: trials are occasions for rejoicing because they produce endurance.
The term can mean either external trial or internal temptation. In vv. 2-4 James uses it for trials; in vv. 13-15 for temptation.
The doubter is like a wave, driven and tossed. The double-minded person is divided in loyalty.

panels.ctx: James writes to Jewish Christians scattered throughout the diaspora. His opening moves immediately to the reality of trials. The logic is clear: trials produce endurance, endurance produces maturity.

cross.note: Paul makes the same connection: suffering produces endurance, endurance produces character.
Peter also connects trials to tested faith.
The LORD gives wisdom; from his mouth come knowledge and understanding.

notes.note: James identifies himself simply as a servant of God and of the Lord Jesus Christ. The twelve tribes in the Dispersion are Jewish Christians.
Count it all joy is a command, not a suggestion. The testing of faith produces steadfastness.
If any lacks wisdom, let him ask God. The promise is generous. But the condition is faith.
Calvin notes that joy in trials is not natural but supernatural. We rejoice not in the suffering itself but in what God accomplishes through it.
The double-minded person wants God gifts without God lordship.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The greeting follows standard epistolary form. The Dispersion originally referred to Jews outside Palestine.
The phrase perfect and complete uses near-synonyms for emphasis.

moo.source: Douglas Moo, Pillar James — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Moo emphasizes that James logic is thoroughly Jewish-wisdom: trials are the furnace in which character is forged.
The request for wisdom connects to the wisdom theme that pervades James.

mccartney.source: Dan McCartney, BECNT James — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: McCartney notes that James draws on the OT tradition of tested faith (Abraham, Job).
Wisdom in James is not abstract knowledge but skill for living faithfully.

sections.header: Verses 9-11 - "The Reversal of Rich and Poor"

heb.paragraph: The lowly brother should boast in his exaltation. James inverts worldly values.
The rich will fade like a flower. The image echoes Isaiah 40:6-8.

panels.ctx: This brief section introduces James critique of wealth that will intensify throughout the letter. The poor brother boasts in exaltation; the rich brother boasts in humiliation.

cross.note: All flesh is grass. The grass withers, the flower fades.
Mary Magnificat: God exalts the humble, sends the rich away empty.

notes.note: The lowly brother boasts in exaltation; the rich brother boasts in humiliation.
The rich man fades away in the midst of his pursuits.
Calvin sees this as a call to right perspective. Both must find their boast in God alone.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase in his humiliation is ironic: the rich man true state is low.

moo.source: Douglas Moo, Pillar James — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Moo notes that James does not condemn wealth per se but the security people place in it.

mccartney.source: Dan McCartney, BECNT James — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: McCartney connects this to the OT prophetic tradition.

sections.header: Verses 12-18 - "Temptation and the Birth of Sin"

heb.paragraph: God cannot be tempted by evil, and he himself tempts no one. God tests but does not tempt.
Each person is tempted when lured and enticed by his own desire. The progression is biological: desire, sin, death.
He brought us forth by the word of truth, that we should be firstfruits of his creatures.

panels.ctx: James now addresses the source of temptation. God is not the author of evil; each person is tempted by their own desire. But God gives only good gifts.

cross.note: Eve saw that the tree was good for food, desirable for wisdom.
The wages of sin is death.
The desires of the flesh, the desires of the eyes, and pride of life.

notes.note: Blessed is the one who endures trial. The crown of life is promised.
God tempts no one. Desire conceives, bears sin, and sin bears death.
God gives only good gifts. He brought us forth by the word of truth.
Calvin stresses human responsibility: we cannot blame God for our sin.
Every good gift descends from above. Regeneration is God initiative.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The verbs lured and enticed are fishing metaphors.
The Father of lights refers to God as creator of celestial bodies.

moo.source: Douglas Moo, Pillar James — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Moo sees this as a crucial theodicy: evil comes from within humans, not from God.

mccartney.source: Dan McCartney, BECNT James — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: McCartney notes the parallel with Genesis 3: desire leads to sin, sin to death.

heb.paragraph: Be doers of the word, not hearers only. The term emphasizes active obedience.
One who looks at himself in a mirror and goes away and forgets.
Pure and undefiled religion is to visit orphans and widows and keep oneself unstained.

panels.ctx: Quick to hear, slow to speak, slow to anger. Be doers of the word, not hearers only. True religion is practical compassion and personal holiness.

cross.note: Everyone who hears these words and does them is like a wise man.
It is not the hearers of the law who are righteous but the doers.
Learn to do good; seek justice, correct oppression.

notes.note: Quick to hear, slow to speak, slow to anger. The implanted word is the gospel.
The mirror analogy: hearing without doing is self-deception.
Religion without tongue-control is worthless. Pure religion is practical.
Calvin notes that Scripture is a mirror showing us our true condition.
True religion has two dimensions: horizontal (care for others) and vertical (holiness).

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The implanted word is the gospel, rooted in the heart at conversion.
The perfect law of liberty is the gospel that liberates from sin.

moo.source: Douglas Moo, Pillar James — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Moo emphasizes that James is not opposing faith to works but dead faith to living faith.

mccartney.source: Dan McCartney, BECNT James — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: McCartney notes that James definition of religion is prophetic, not priestly.

themes.note: Faith dominates as James introduces his thesis: faith must be lived.

rows.label: vv. 1-8
rows.text: Greeting; joy in trials; asking for wisdom

rows.label: vv. 12-18
rows.text: Temptation, sin, and God good gifts

rows.label: vv. 19-27
rows.text: Be doers of the word; pure religion

lit.note: Chapter 1 introduces the letter major themes: trials, wisdom, rich/poor reversal, temptation, and obedient action.
